questions.question: Who is considered the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the fundamental structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Who first saw and described a live cell?
questions.explanation: Antonie Von Leeuwenhoek first saw and described a live cell.

questions.question: Who discovered the nucleus of a cell?
questions.explanation: Robert Brown discovered the nucleus of the cell.

questions.question: Organisms composed of a single cell are called:
questions.explanation: Organisms composed of a single cell are called unicellular organisms.

questions.question: Which instrument revealed all the structural details of the cell?
questions.explanation: The invention and improvement of the microscope leading to the electron microscope revealed all the structural details of the cell.

questions.question: In which year did Matthias Schleiden examine plants and propose that all plants are made of cells?
questions.explanation: In 1838, Matthias Schleiden, a German botanist, examined a large number of plants and observed that all plants are composed of different kinds of cells.

questions.question: Matthias Schleiden was a:
questions.explanation: Matthias Schleiden was a German botanist who examined plants and contributed to cell theory.

questions.question: Theodore Schwann was a:
questions.explanation: Theodore Schwann (1839) was a German Zoologist who studied different types of animal cells.

questions.question: Who proposed that the bodies of animals and plants are composed of cells and products of cells?
questions.explanation: Schwann proposed the hypothesis that the bodies of animals and plants are composed of cells and products of cells, based on his studies.

questions.question: Who together formulated the cell theory?
questions.explanation: Schleiden and Schwann together formulated the cell theory.

questions.question: The original cell theory formulated by Schleiden and Schwann did NOT explain:

options.A: That all plants are made of cells
options.B: That all animals are made of cells
options.D: That cells are units of life

questions.explanation: The original cell theory did not explain as to how new cells were formed.

questions.question: Rudolf Virchow explained that new cells arise from:
questions.explanation: Rudolf Virchow (1855) first explained that cells divided and new cells are formed from pre-existing cells (Omnis cellula-e cellula).

questions.question: What does the Latin phrase 'Omnis cellula-e cellula' mean?

options.B: All cells arise from pre-existing cells

questions.explanation: 'Omnis cellula-e cellula' means all cells arise from pre-existing cells, as stated by Rudolf Virchow.

questions.question: In which year did Rudolf Virchow modify and complete the cell theory?
questions.explanation: Rudolf Virchow in 1855 explained that cells divide and new cells form from pre-existing cells, completing the cell theory.

questions.question: According to modern cell theory, which of the following is correct?

options.A: All living organisms have a nucleus
options.B: All cells arise from pre-existing cells
options.C: New cells can arise from abiotic material

questions.explanation: Modern cell theory states: (i) all living organisms are composed of cells, and (ii) all cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: Theodore Schwann studied animal cells and reported that cells had a thin outer layer. This layer is now known as:
questions.explanation: Schwann studied animal cells and reported that cells had a thin outer layer which is today known as the 'plasma membrane'.

questions.question: Schwann concluded that cell wall is a unique character of:
questions.explanation: Schwann concluded, based on his studies on plant tissues, that the presence of cell wall is a unique character of the plant cells.

questions.question: The onion cell has a distinct cell wall as its outer boundary. Just within it is the:
questions.explanation: The onion cell, a typical plant cell, has a distinct cell wall as its outer boundary and just within it is the cell membrane.

questions.question: The nucleus contains chromosomes which in turn contain the genetic material:
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains the chromosomes which in turn contain the genetic material, DNA.

questions.question: Cells that have membrane-bound nuclei are called:
questions.explanation: Cells that have membrane-bound nuclei are called eukaryotic.

questions.question: Cells that lack a membrane-bound nucleus are called:
questions.explanation: Cells that lack a membrane-bound nucleus are called prokaryotic.

questions.question: The semi-fluid matrix that occupies the volume of the cell in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells is called:
questions.explanation: In both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, a semi-fluid matrix called cytoplasm occupies the volume of the cell.

questions.question: The cytoplasm is the main arena of cellular activities in:
questions.explanation: The cytoplasm is the main arena of cellular activities in both the plant and animal cells.

questions.question: Which of the following organelles are NOT found in prokaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria, golgi complex, lysosomes, etc. Only ribosomes are found in both.

questions.question: Which organelle is found in all cells — both eukaryotic and prokaryotic?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are non-membrane-bound organelles found in all cells – both eukaryotic as well as prokaryotic.

questions.question: Within eukaryotic cells, ribosomes are found in which organelles?
questions.explanation: Within the cell, ribosomes are found not only in the cytoplasm but also within two organelles – chloroplasts (in plants) and mitochondria and on rough ER.

questions.question: Which non-membrane-bound organelle is found in animal cells and helps in cell division?
questions.explanation: Animal cells contain another non-membrane-bound organelle called centrosome which contains centrioles and helps in cell division.

questions.question: The smallest cells known are:
questions.explanation: Mycoplasmas are the smallest cells, only 0.3 µm in length.

questions.question: The largest isolated single cell is:
questions.explanation: The largest isolated single cell is the egg of an ostrich.

questions.question: Human red blood cells are about how many micrometres in diameter?
questions.explanation: Among multicellular organisms, human red blood cells are about 7.0 µm in diameter.

questions.question: Which cells are some of the longest cells in the human body?
questions.explanation: Nerve cells are some of the longest cells.

questions.question: Bacteria could be __ µm in length:
questions.explanation: Bacteria could be 3 to 5 µm in length.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a prokaryotic organism?
questions.explanation: Amoeba is a eukaryotic organism. Prokaryotes include bacteria, blue-green algae, mycoplasma, and PPLO.

questions.question: PPLO stands for:
questions.explanation: PPLO stands for Pleuro Pneumonia Like Organisms.

questions.question: Prokaryotic cells are generally __ than eukaryotic cells:
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller and multiply more rapidly than the eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: The rod-like shape of bacteria is called:
questions.explanation: The four basic shapes of bacteria are bacillus (rod like), coccus (spherical), vibrio (comma shaped) and spirillum (spiral).

questions.question: Spherical bacteria are called:
questions.explanation: Coccus refers to spherical-shaped bacteria.

questions.question: Comma-shaped bacteria are called:
questions.explanation: Vibrio refers to comma-shaped bacteria.

questions.question: Spiral-shaped bacteria are called:
questions.explanation: Spirillum refers to spiral-shaped bacteria.

questions.question: In prokaryotes, there is no well-defined nucleus. The genetic material is:

options.B: Basically naked, not enveloped by nuclear membrane

questions.explanation: In prokaryotes, the genetic material is basically naked, not enveloped by a nuclear membrane.

questions.question: Plasmids are:

options.B: Small circular DNA outside genomic DNA in bacteria

questions.explanation: Many bacteria have small circular DNA outside the genomic DNA. These smaller DNA are called plasmids.

questions.question: Plasmid DNA confers which unique phenotypic character to bacteria?
questions.explanation: Plasmid DNA confers certain unique phenotypic characters to bacteria. One such character is resistance to antibiotics.

questions.question: The cell wall of most prokaryotes (except mycoplasma) surrounds the:
questions.explanation: All prokaryotes have a cell wall surrounding the cell membrane except in mycoplasma.

questions.question: A specialised differentiated form of cell membrane called mesosome is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: A specialised differentiated form of cell membrane called mesosome is the characteristic of prokaryotes.

questions.question: The cell envelope of prokaryotes consists of how many layers?
questions.explanation: The cell envelope consists of a tightly bound three-layered structure: the outermost glycocalyx, the cell wall, and then the plasma membrane.

questions.question: The outermost layer of the prokaryotic cell envelope is:
questions.explanation: The cell envelope consists of the outermost glycocalyx, followed by the cell wall, and then the plasma membrane.

questions.question: Bacteria that take up the gram stain are called:
questions.explanation: Bacteria that take up the gram stain are Gram positive and those that do not are called Gram negative bacteria.

questions.question: A loose sheath of glycocalyx in some bacteria is called:
questions.explanation: Glycocalyx could be a loose sheath called the slime layer in some bacteria.

questions.question: A thick and tough glycocalyx in some bacteria is called:
questions.explanation: In some bacteria, the glycocalyx may be thick and tough, called the capsule.

questions.question: The cell wall of bacteria:

options.A: Determines the shape of the cell and provides structural support

questions.explanation: The cell wall determines the shape of the cell and provides a strong structural support to prevent the bacterium from bursting or collapsing.

questions.question: The plasma membrane of prokaryotes is:
questions.explanation: The plasma membrane is selectively permeable in nature and interacts with the outside world. This membrane is similar structurally to that of the eukaryotes.

questions.question: Mesosomes are formed by extensions of:
questions.explanation: The mesosome is formed by the extensions of plasma membrane into the cell.

questions.question: Mesosomes are in the form of:
questions.explanation: The extensions (mesosomes) are in the form of vesicles, tubules and lamellae.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of mesosomes?
questions.explanation: Mesosomes help in cell wall formation, DNA replication, distribution to daughter cells, respiration, secretion, and increasing surface area. Protein synthesis is done by ribosomes.

questions.question: Mesosomes help to increase the __ of the plasma membrane:
questions.explanation: Mesosomes help in respiration, secretion processes, to increase the surface area of the plasma membrane and enzymatic content.

questions.question: In some prokaryotes like cyanobacteria, membranous extensions into the cytoplasm containing pigments are called:
questions.explanation: In some prokaryotes like cyanobacteria, there are other membranous extensions into the cytoplasm called chromatophores which contain pigments.

questions.question: Motile bacteria have thin filamentous extensions from their cell wall called:
questions.explanation: If motile, bacteria have thin filamentous extensions from their cell wall called flagella.

questions.question: The bacterial flagellum is composed of how many parts?
questions.explanation: Bacterial flagellum is composed of three parts – filament, hook and basal body.

questions.question: The longest part of the bacterial flagellum that extends from cell surface to outside is the:
questions.explanation: The filament is the longest portion and extends from the cell surface to the outside.

questions.question: Pili and Fimbriae are surface structures of bacteria that:

options.B: Do NOT play a role in motility

questions.explanation: Pili and Fimbriae are also surface structures of the bacteria but do not play a role in motility.

questions.question: Pili are:

options.A: Small bristle-like fibres made of carbohydrates
options.B: Elongated tubular structures made of a special protein
options.D: Membranous extensions of the plasma membrane

questions.explanation: The pili are elongated tubular structures made of a special protein.

questions.question: Fimbriae are:

options.B: Small bristle-like fibres sprouting out of the cell

questions.explanation: The fimbriae are small bristle-like fibres sprouting out of the cell.

questions.question: In some bacteria, fimbriae are known to help:

options.B: Attach bacteria to rocks and host tissues

questions.explanation: In some bacteria, fimbriae are known to help attach the bacteria to rocks in streams and also to the host tissues.

questions.question: In prokaryotes, ribosomes are associated with the:
questions.explanation: In prokaryotes, ribosomes are associated with the plasma membrane of the cell.

questions.question: Prokaryotic ribosomes are of which type?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S, made of 50S and 30S subunits.

questions.question: Prokaryotic ribosomes are made of which two subunits?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic ribosomes are made of two subunits - 50S and 30S units which when present together form 70S prokaryotic ribosomes.

questions.question: Ribosomes are the site of:
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis.

questions.question: Several ribosomes attached to a single mRNA form a chain called:
questions.explanation: Several ribosomes may attach to a single mRNA and form a chain called polyribosomes or polysome.

questions.question: Reserve material in prokaryotic cells is stored in the cytoplasm as:
questions.explanation: Reserve material in prokaryotic cells are stored in the cytoplasm in the form of inclusion bodies.

questions.question: Inclusion bodies in prokaryotes are:

options.B: Not bound by any membrane system

questions.explanation: Inclusion bodies are not bound by any membrane system and lie free in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: Gas vacuoles are found in which bacteria?

options.B: Blue-green and purple and green photosynthetic bacteria

questions.explanation: Gas vacuoles are found in blue-green and purple and green photosynthetic bacteria.

questions.question: Eukaryotes include:

options.C: All protists, plants, animals and fungi

questions.explanation: The eukaryotes include all the protists, plants, animals and fungi.

questions.question: Eukaryotic cells have extensive compartmentalization of cytoplasm through:
questions.explanation: In eukaryotic cells there is an extensive compartmentalization of cytoplasm through the presence of membrane-bound organelles.

questions.question: Genetic material in eukaryotes is organised into:
questions.explanation: Their genetic material is organised into chromosomes.

questions.question: Which structures are present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?

options.B: Cell walls, plastids, and large central vacuole

questions.explanation: Plant cells possess cell walls, plastids and a large central vacuole which are absent in animal cells.

questions.question: Centrioles are present in animal cells but absent in:
questions.explanation: Animal cells have centrioles which are absent in almost all plant cells.

questions.question: The detailed structure of the cell membrane was studied after the advent of the electron microscope in the:
questions.explanation: The detailed structure of the membrane was studied only after the advent of the electron microscope in the 1950s.

questions.question: The cell membrane is mainly composed of:
questions.explanation: These studies showed that the cell membrane is mainly composed of lipids and proteins.

questions.question: The major lipids in the cell membrane are arranged in:
questions.explanation: The major lipids are phospholipids that are arranged in a bilayer.

questions.question: In the cell membrane, the polar heads of phospholipids face:

options.C: Both inner and outer but randomly
options.D: Towards each other in the middle

questions.explanation: The lipids are arranged within the membrane with the polar head towards the outer sides and the hydrophobic tails towards the inner part.

questions.question: The hydrophobic tails in the phospholipid bilayer face:
questions.explanation: The hydrophobic tails face towards the inner part, which ensures that the nonpolar tail of saturated hydrocarbons is protected from the aqueous environment.

questions.question: In addition to phospholipids, membrane also contains:
questions.explanation: In addition to phospholipids, membrane also contains cholesterol.

questions.question: The membrane of human erythrocyte has approximately what percentage of protein?
questions.explanation: The membrane of the erythrocyte has approximately 52 per cent protein and 40 per cent lipids.

questions.question: Membrane proteins that lie on the surface of the membrane are called:
questions.explanation: Peripheral proteins lie on the surface of membrane while the integral proteins are partially or totally buried in the membrane.

questions.question: Membrane proteins that are partially or totally buried in the membrane are called:
questions.explanation: Integral proteins are partially or totally buried in the membrane.

questions.question: The fluid mosaic model of cell membrane was proposed by:
questions.explanation: An improved model of the structure of cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972) widely accepted as fluid mosaic model.

questions.question: In which year was the fluid mosaic model proposed?
questions.explanation: Singer and Nicolson proposed the fluid mosaic model in 1972.

questions.question: According to the fluid mosaic model, the quasi-fluid nature of lipid enables:

options.B: Lateral movement of proteins within the bilayer

questions.explanation: According to the fluid mosaic model, the quasi-fluid nature of lipid enables lateral movement of proteins within the overall bilayer.

questions.question: The ability of proteins to move within the membrane is measured as its:
questions.explanation: The ability to move within the membrane is measured as its fluidity.

questions.question: Movement of molecules across the plasma membrane without energy is called:
questions.explanation: Many molecules can move briefly across the membrane without any requirement of energy and this is called the passive transport.

questions.question: Neutral solutes move across the membrane by the process of:
questions.explanation: Neutral solutes may move across the membrane by the process of simple diffusion along the concentration gradient.

questions.question: Movement of water by diffusion is called:
questions.explanation: Water may also move across this membrane from higher to lower concentration. Movement of water by diffusion is called osmosis.

questions.question: Polar molecules require __ to be transported across the membrane:
questions.explanation: As the polar molecules cannot pass through the nonpolar lipid bilayer, they require a carrier protein of the membrane to facilitate their transport across the membrane.

questions.question: Transport of molecules from lower to higher concentration is called:
questions.explanation: A few ions or molecules are transported against their concentration gradient (lower to higher) which is energy-dependent and called active transport.

questions.question: Which energy currency is utilized in active transport?
questions.explanation: Active transport is an energy-dependent process, in which ATP is utilised.

questions.question: Na+/K+ Pump is an example of:
questions.explanation: Active transport, e.g., Na+/K+ Pump, is energy-dependent and transports molecules against concentration gradient.

questions.question: Cell wall is a non-living rigid structure found in:
questions.explanation: A non-living rigid structure called the cell wall forms an outer covering for the plasma membrane of fungi and plants.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the cell wall?
questions.explanation: Cell wall gives shape, protects from damage and infection, helps in cell-to-cell interaction, and provides barrier to macromolecules. Protein synthesis occurs at ribosomes.

questions.question: The cell wall of algae is made of:

options.B: Cellulose, galactans, mannans and minerals like calcium carbonate

questions.explanation: Algae have cell wall, made of cellulose, galactans, mannans and minerals like calcium carbonate.

questions.question: In other plants (non-algae), the cell wall consists of:
questions.explanation: In other plants, the cell wall consists of cellulose, hemicellulose, pectins and proteins.

questions.question: The primary wall of a young plant cell:

options.C: Is formed on the inner side

questions.explanation: The cell wall of a young plant cell, the primary wall is capable of growth, which gradually diminishes as the cell matures.

questions.question: The secondary wall is formed on the __ side of the cell:
questions.explanation: The secondary wall is formed on the inner (towards membrane) side of the cell.

questions.question: The middle lamella is mainly composed of:
questions.explanation: The middle lamella is a layer mainly of calcium pectate which holds or glues the different neighbouring cells together.

questions.question: Plasmodesmata connect the cytoplasm of:
questions.explanation: The cell wall and middle lamellae may be traversed by plasmodesmata which connect the cytoplasm of neighbouring cells.

questions.question: The endomembrane system includes:

options.A: ER, Golgi complex, mitochondria and vacuoles
options.B: ER, Golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles

questions.explanation: The endomembrane system includes endoplasmic reticulum (ER), golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the endomembrane system?
questions.explanation: Since the functions of the mitochondria, chloroplast and peroxisomes are not coordinated with the endomembrane components, these are not considered part of the endomembrane system.

questions.question: The endoplasmic reticulum is a network of tiny tubular structures scattered in:
questions.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a network or reticulum of tiny tubular structures scattered in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: ER divides the intracellular space into which two compartments?
questions.explanation: ER divides the intracellular space into two distinct compartments, i.e., luminal (inside ER) and extra luminal (cytoplasm) compartments.

questions.question: Endoplasmic reticulum bearing ribosomes on their surface is called:
questions.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum bearing ribosomes on their surface is called rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER).

questions.question: Endoplasmic reticulum without ribosomes is called:
questions.explanation: In the absence of ribosomes they appear smooth and are called smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER).

questions.question: RER is frequently observed in cells actively involved in:
questions.explanation: RER is frequently observed in the cells actively involved in protein synthesis and secretion.

questions.question: RER is extensive and continuous with:
questions.explanation: RER is extensive and continuous with the outer membrane of the nucleus.

questions.question: The smooth endoplasmic reticulum is the major site for synthesis of:
questions.explanation: The smooth endoplasmic reticulum is the major site for synthesis of lipid.

questions.question: In animal cells, lipid-like steroidal hormones are synthesized in:
questions.explanation: In animal cells lipid-like steroidal hormones are synthesised in SER.

questions.question: Who first observed densely stained reticular structures near the nucleus (later named Golgi bodies)?
questions.explanation: Camillo Golgi (1898) first observed densely stained reticular structures near the nucleus. These were later named Golgi bodies after him.

questions.question: In which year did Camillo Golgi first observe the Golgi bodies?
questions.explanation: Camillo Golgi (1898) first observed densely stained reticular structures near the nucleus.

questions.question: Golgi bodies consist of many flat, disc-shaped sacs called:
questions.explanation: Golgi bodies consist of many flat, disc-shaped sacs or cisternae of 0.5µm to 1.0µm diameter.

questions.question: The diameter of Golgi cisternae is:
questions.explanation: Golgi cisternae are 0.5µm to 1.0µm in diameter.

questions.question: The convex face of the Golgi apparatus near the nucleus is called:
questions.explanation: The Golgi cisternae are concentrically arranged near the nucleus with distinct convex cis or the forming face.

questions.question: The concave face of the Golgi apparatus is called:
questions.explanation: The concave face is called trans or the maturing face.

questions.question: The principal function of the Golgi apparatus is:

options.C: Packaging materials for delivery to intracellular targets or secretion

questions.explanation: The golgi apparatus principally performs the function of packaging materials, to be delivered either to the intracellular targets or secreted outside the cell.

questions.question: Materials to be packaged from ER fuse with the __ face of the golgi apparatus:
questions.explanation: Materials to be packaged in the form of vesicles from the ER fuse with the cis face of the golgi apparatus and move towards the maturing face.

questions.question: Proteins synthesized by ribosomes on ER are modified in the cisternae of Golgi before being released from its:
questions.explanation: A number of proteins synthesised by ribosomes on the endoplasmic reticulum are modified in the cisternae of the golgi apparatus before they are released from its trans face.

questions.question: Golgi apparatus is the important site of formation of:
questions.explanation: Golgi apparatus is the important site of formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

questions.question: Lysosomes are formed by the process of packaging in the:
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicular structures formed by the process of packaging in the golgi apparatus.

questions.question: Lysosomes contain which types of enzymes?
questions.explanation: The isolated lysosomal vesicles have been found to be very rich in almost all types of hydrolytic enzymes (hydrolases – lipases, proteases, carbohydrases).

questions.question: Lysosomal enzymes are optimally active at:
questions.explanation: Lysosomal enzymes are optimally active at the acidic pH.

questions.question: Lysosomal enzymes are capable of digesting:

options.C: Carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids

questions.explanation: These enzymes are capable of digesting carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids.

questions.question: The vacuole is a membrane-bound space found in the:
questions.explanation: The vacuole is the membrane-bound space found in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: The single membrane that bounds the vacuole is called:
questions.explanation: The vacuole is bound by a single membrane called tonoplast.

questions.question: In plant cells, vacuoles can occupy up to what percentage of the cell volume?
questions.explanation: In plant cells the vacuoles can occupy up to 90 per cent of the volume of the cell.

questions.question: In plants, the tonoplast facilitates transport of ions into the vacuole against concentration gradient, hence their concentration is:
questions.explanation: In plants, the tonoplast facilitates the transport of a number of ions and other materials against concentration gradients into the vacuole, hence their concentration is significantly higher in the vacuole than in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: In Amoeba, the contractile vacuole is important for:
questions.explanation: In Amoeba, the contractile vacuole is important for osmoregulation and excretion.

questions.question: Food vacuoles in protists are formed by:
questions.explanation: In many cells, as in protists, food vacuoles are formed by engulfing the food particles.

questions.question: Mitochondria are the sites of:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the sites of aerobic respiration.

questions.question: Mitochondria are called the 'power houses' of the cell because they:

options.B: Produce cellular energy in the form of ATP

questions.explanation: Mitochondria produce cellular energy in the form of ATP, hence they are called 'power houses' of the cell.

questions.question: The typical shape of mitochondria is:
questions.explanation: Typically mitochondrion is sausage-shaped or cylindrical.

questions.question: The average diameter of a mitochondrion is:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria typically have a diameter of 0.2-1.0 µm (average 0.5 µm) and length 1.0-4.1 µm.

questions.question: Each mitochondrion is a:
questions.explanation: Each mitochondrion is a double membrane-bound structure.

questions.question: The inner compartment of mitochondria is filled with a dense homogeneous substance called:
questions.explanation: The inner compartment is filled with a dense homogeneous substance called the matrix.

questions.question: The infoldings of the inner membrane of mitochondria are called:
questions.explanation: The inner membrane forms a number of infoldings called the cristae (sing.: crista) towards the matrix.

questions.question: The function of cristae is to:
questions.explanation: The cristae increase the surface area.

questions.question: The mitochondrial matrix possesses:

options.B: Single circular DNA, RNA, and 70S ribosomes

questions.explanation: The matrix possesses single circular DNA molecule, a few RNA molecules, ribosomes (70S) and the components required for the synthesis of proteins.

questions.question: Mitochondria divide by:
questions.explanation: The mitochondria divide by fission.

questions.question: Plastids are found in:

options.B: All plant cells and in euglenoides

questions.explanation: Plastids are found in all plant cells and in euglenoides.

questions.question: Plastids bearing chlorophyll and carotenoid pigments are called:
questions.explanation: The chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and carotenoid pigments which are responsible for trapping light energy essential for photosynthesis.

questions.question: Chromoplasts contain fat-soluble carotenoid pigments that impart __ colour to plants:
questions.explanation: In the chromoplasts, fat-soluble carotenoid pigments like carotene, xanthophylls and others are present. This gives the part of the plant a yellow, orange or red colour.

questions.question: Colourless plastids with stored nutrients are called:
questions.explanation: The leucoplasts are the colourless plastids of varied shapes and sizes with stored nutrients.

questions.question: Amyloplasts store:
questions.explanation: Amyloplasts store carbohydrates (starch), e.g., potato.

questions.question: Elaioplasts store:
questions.explanation: Elaioplasts store oils and fats.

questions.question: Aleuroplasts store:
questions.explanation: The aleuroplasts store proteins.

questions.question: Majority of chloroplasts of green plants are found in:
questions.explanation: Majority of the chloroplasts of the green plants are found in the mesophyll cells of the leaves.

questions.question: Chloroplasts are which membrane-bound structure?
questions.explanation: Like mitochondria, the chloroplasts are also double membrane-bound.

questions.question: The space limited by the inner membrane of the chloroplast is called:
questions.explanation: The space limited by the inner membrane of the chloroplast is called the stroma.

questions.question: Organized flattened membranous sacs present in the stroma of chloroplasts are called:
questions.explanation: A number of organised flattened membranous sacs called the thylakoids, are present in the stroma.

questions.question: Thylakoids arranged in stacks like piles of coins are called:
questions.explanation: Thylakoids are arranged in stacks like the piles of coins called grana (singular: granum).

questions.question: Flat membranous tubules connecting thylakoids of different grana are called:
questions.explanation: There are flat membranous tubules called the stroma lamellae connecting the thylakoids of the different grana.

questions.question: The membrane of thylakoids encloses a space called:
questions.explanation: The membrane of the thylakoids enclose a space called a lumen.

questions.question: The stroma of chloroplast contains enzymes required for synthesis of:
questions.explanation: The stroma of the chloroplast contains enzymes required for the synthesis of carbohydrates and proteins.

questions.question: Chlorophyll pigments are present in the:
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll pigments are present in the thylakoids.

questions.question: The ribosomes of chloroplasts are __ than the cytoplasmic ribosomes:
questions.explanation: The ribosomes of the chloroplasts are smaller (70S) than the cytoplasmic ribosomes (80S).

questions.question: The number of chloroplasts per cell in Chlamydomonas (a green alga) is:
questions.explanation: Their number varies from 1 per cell of the Chlamydomonas, a green alga to 20-40 per cell in the mesophyll.

questions.question: Ribosomes were first observed under the electron microscope as dense particles by:
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the granular structures first observed under the electron microscope as dense particles by George Palade (1953).

questions.question: In which year did George Palade first observe ribosomes?
questions.explanation: George Palade (1953) first observed ribosomes as dense particles under electron microscope.

questions.question: Ribosomes are composed of:
questions.explanation: They are composed of ribonucleic acid (RNA) and proteins and are not surrounded by any membrane.

questions.question: Eukaryotic ribosomes are:
questions.explanation: The eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S while the prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S.

questions.question: The two subunits of 80S ribosomes are:
questions.explanation: The two subunits of 80S ribosomes are 60S and 40S.

questions.question: 'S' in ribosome classification stands for:
questions.explanation: Here 'S' (Svedberg's Unit) stands for the sedimentation coefficient; it is indirectly a measure of density and size.

questions.question: The cytoskeleton consists of:
questions.explanation: An elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures consisting of microtubules, microfilaments and intermediate filaments present in the cytoplasm is collectively referred to as the cytoskeleton.

questions.question: Functions of cytoskeleton include:

options.B: Mechanical support, motility, maintenance of shape

questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton in a cell are involved in many functions such as mechanical support, motility, maintenance of the shape of the cell.

questions.question: Cilia and flagella are hair-like outgrowths of the:
questions.explanation: Cilia (sing.: cilium) and flagella (sing.: flagellum) are hair-like outgrowths of the cell membrane.

questions.question: Cilia work like oars causing movement of:

options.B: Either the cell or the surrounding fluid

questions.explanation: Cilia are small structures which work like oars, causing the movement of either the cell or the surrounding fluid.

questions.question: Compared to cilia, flagella are:
questions.explanation: Flagella are comparatively longer and responsible for cell movement.

questions.question: The core of a cilium or flagellum is called:
questions.explanation: Their core called the axoneme, possesses a number of microtubules running parallel to the long axis.

questions.question: The arrangement of axonemal microtubules in cilia/flagella is referred to as the:
questions.explanation: The axoneme usually has nine doublets of radially arranged peripheral microtubules, and a pair of centrally located microtubules. Such an arrangement is referred to as the 9+2 array.

questions.question: The central tubules in cilia/flagella are connected by:
questions.explanation: The central tubules are connected by bridges and is also enclosed by a central sheath.

questions.question: The central sheath of cilia is connected to one of the tubules of each peripheral doublet by:
questions.explanation: The central sheath is connected to one of the tubules of each peripheral doublets by a radial spoke. Thus, there are nine radial spokes.

questions.question: Both the cilium and flagellum emerge from centriole-like structures called:
questions.explanation: Both the cilium and flagellum emerge from centriole-like structure called the basal bodies.

questions.question: A centrosome usually contains how many cylindrical structures called centrioles?
questions.explanation: Centrosome is an organelle usually containing two cylindrical structures called centrioles.

questions.question: The two centrioles in a centrosome lie __ to each other:
questions.explanation: Both the centrioles in a centrosome lie perpendicular to each other in which each has an organisation like the cartwheel.

questions.question: Centrioles are made up of nine evenly spaced peripheral fibrils of:
questions.explanation: Centrioles are made up of nine evenly spaced peripheral fibrils of tubulin protein. Each of the peripheral fibril is a triplet.

questions.question: Each peripheral fibril of a centriole is a:
questions.explanation: Each of the peripheral fibril is a triplet. The adjacent triplets are also linked.

questions.question: The central part of the proximal region of the centriole is called:
questions.explanation: The central part of the proximal region of the centriole is also proteinaceous and called the hub, which is connected with tubules of the peripheral triplets by radial spokes made of protein.

questions.question: During cell division in animal cells, centrioles form the:
questions.explanation: The centrioles form the basal body of cilia or flagella, and spindle fibres that give rise to spindle apparatus during cell division in animal cells.

questions.question: Nucleus as a cell organelle was first described by Robert Brown in:
questions.explanation: Nucleus as a cell organelle was first described by Robert Brown as early as 1831.

questions.question: The material of the nucleus stained by basic dyes was given the name 'chromatin' by:
questions.explanation: Later the material of the nucleus stained by the basic dyes was given the name chromatin by Flemming.

questions.question: The interphase nucleus has highly extended nucleoprotein fibres called:
questions.explanation: The interphase nucleus has highly extended and elaborate nucleoprotein fibres called chromatin, nuclear matrix and one or more spherical bodies called nucleoli.

questions.question: The nuclear envelope consists of __ parallel membranes with a space called perinuclear space between them:
questions.explanation: The nuclear envelope consists of two parallel membranes with a space between (10 to 50 nm) called the perinuclear space.

questions.question: The perinuclear space is:
questions.explanation: The nuclear envelope consists of two parallel membranes with a space between (10 to 50 nm) called the perinuclear space.

questions.question: The outer membrane of nuclear envelope usually remains continuous with:
questions.explanation: The outer membrane usually remains continuous with the endoplasmic reticulum and also bears ribosomes on it.

questions.question: Nuclear pores are formed by the fusion of the:
questions.explanation: At a number of places the nuclear envelope is interrupted by minute pores, which are formed by the fusion of its two membranes.

questions.question: Nuclear pores serve as passages for movement of:

options.C: RNA and protein molecules in both directions

questions.explanation: These nuclear pores are the passages through which movement of RNA and protein molecules takes place in both directions between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.

questions.question: Normally there is how many nucleus per cell?
questions.explanation: Normally, there is only one nucleus per cell, variations in the number of nuclei are also frequently observed.

questions.question: Which mature cells lack nucleus?

options.B: Erythrocytes of many mammals and sieve tube cells of vascular plants

questions.explanation: Some mature cells even lack nucleus, e.g., erythrocytes of many mammals and sieve tube cells of vascular plants.

questions.question: The nucleoli are:
questions.explanation: The content of nucleolus is continuous with the rest of the nucleoplasm as it is not a membrane-bound structure.

questions.question: Nucleolus is a site for active synthesis of:
questions.explanation: It (nucleolus) is a site for active ribosomal RNA synthesis.

questions.question: Larger and more numerous nucleoli are present in cells actively carrying out:
questions.explanation: Larger and more numerous nucleoli are present in cells actively carrying out protein synthesis.

questions.question: Chromatin contains:

options.B: DNA, histones, non-histone proteins, and RNA

questions.explanation: Chromatin contains DNA and some basic proteins called histones, some non-histone proteins and also RNA.

questions.question: A single human cell has approximately how long a thread of DNA distributed among 46 chromosomes?
questions.explanation: A single human cell has approximately two metre long thread of DNA distributed among its forty six (twenty three pairs) chromosomes.

questions.question: The primary constriction of a chromosome is called the:
questions.explanation: Every chromosome essentially has a primary constriction or the centromere on the sides of which disc shaped structures called kinetochores are present.

questions.question: Disc-shaped structures present on the sides of the centromere are called:
questions.explanation: Disc-shaped structures called kinetochores are present on the sides of the centromere.

questions.question: The centromere holds together the:
questions.explanation: Centromere holds two chromatids of a chromosome.

questions.question: A chromosome with the centromere in the middle, forming two equal arms, is called:
questions.explanation: The metacentric chromosome has middle centromere forming two equal arms of the chromosome.

questions.question: A chromosome with centromere slightly away from the middle, resulting in one shorter and one longer arm, is called:
questions.explanation: The sub-metacentric chromosome has centromere slightly away from the middle of the chromosome resulting into one shorter arm and one longer arm.

questions.question: A chromosome with centromere close to its end, forming one extremely short and one very long arm, is called:
questions.explanation: In case of acrocentric chromosome the centromere is situated close to its end forming one extremely short and one very long arm.

questions.question: A chromosome with a terminal centromere is called:
questions.explanation: The telocentric chromosome has a terminal centromere.

questions.question: A small fragment that appears when chromosomes have non-staining secondary constrictions at constant locations is called:
questions.explanation: Sometimes a few chromosomes have non-staining secondary constrictions at a constant location. This gives the appearance of a small fragment called the satellite.

questions.question: Microbodies are membrane-bound minute vesicles that contain:
questions.explanation: Many membrane-bound minute vesicles called microbodies that contain various enzymes, are present in both plant and animal cells.

questions.question: Microbodies are found in:
questions.explanation: Many membrane-bound minute vesicles called microbodies are present in both plant and animal cells.

questions.question: Which cellular membrane is selectively permeable?
questions.explanation: One of the most important functions of the plasma membrane is the transport of molecules across it. The membrane is selectively permeable.

questions.question: Nuclear membrane is found in:
questions.explanation: Nuclear membrane is found in eukaryotes. Prokaryotes lack a nuclear membrane.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is part of cell theory as understood today?

options.C: All living organisms are composed of cells and products of cells
options.D: Only plants are made of cells

questions.explanation: Cell theory states: (i) all living organisms are composed of cells and products of cells, and (ii) all cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: The endomembrane system components have functions that are:
questions.explanation: Many membranous organelles are considered together as an endomembrane system because their functions are coordinated.

questions.question: The fluid mosaic model explains why the membrane can perform functions like:

options.A: Cell growth, secretion, endocytosis, cell division

questions.explanation: The fluid nature of the membrane is important from the point of view of functions like cell growth, formation of intercellular junctions, secretion, endocytosis, cell division etc.

questions.question: In prokaryotes, the single chromosome is:
questions.explanation: In addition to the genomic DNA (the single chromosome/circular DNA), many bacteria have small circular DNA outside the genomic DNA.

questions.question: The two aqueous compartments of mitochondria are:
questions.explanation: The two membranes divide the mitochondrial lumen distinctly into two aqueous compartments, i.e., the outer compartment and the inner compartment.

questions.question: The Golgi apparatus remains in close association with the endoplasmic reticulum because:

options.A: Both are made of same membrane
options.B: Vesicles from ER fuse with cis face of Golgi

questions.explanation: Materials to be packaged in the form of vesicles from the ER fuse with the cis face of the golgi apparatus, which explains why the golgi apparatus remains in close association with the endoplasmic reticulum.

questions.question: What does the vacuole contain?

options.B: Water, sap, excretory products and other materials not useful for the cell

questions.explanation: The vacuole contains water, sap, excretory product and other materials not useful for the cell.

questions.question: Based on the type of pigments, plastids can be classified into how many types?
questions.explanation: Based on the type of pigments plastids can be classified into chloroplasts, chromoplasts and leucoplasts — three types.

questions.question: In chloroplasts, the inner chloroplast membrane is:
questions.explanation: Of the two membranes, the inner chloroplast membrane is relatively less permeable.

questions.question: Which statement about prokaryotic cells is INCORRECT?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells do NOT have a membrane-bound nucleus. They lack nuclear envelope.

questions.question: Simple diffusion occurs along the:

options.B: Along concentration gradient (from higher to lower)

questions.explanation: Neutral solutes may move across the membrane by the process of simple diffusion along the concentration gradient, i.e., from higher concentration to the lower.

questions.question: During cell division, cells show structured chromosomes in place of the nucleus. During interphase there is:

options.B: Loose and indistinct network of nucleoprotein fibres (chromatin)

questions.explanation: The interphase nucleus has a loose and indistinct network of nucleoprotein fibres called chromatin.

questions.question: A typical eukaryotic cell consists of:
questions.explanation: A typical eukaryotic cell consists of a cell membrane, nucleus and cytoplasm.

questions.question: Why are mitochondria, chloroplasts, and peroxisomes NOT considered part of the endomembrane system?

options.B: Their functions are not coordinated with ER, Golgi, lysosomes and vacuoles
options.D: They are only in plant cells

questions.explanation: Since the functions of the mitochondria, chloroplast and peroxisomes are not coordinated with the above components, these are not considered as part of the endomembrane system.

questions.question: In the phospholipid bilayer, which part is protected from the aqueous environment?
questions.explanation: The arrangement ensures that the nonpolar tail of saturated hydrocarbons is protected from the aqueous environment.

questions.question: Prokaryotic ribosomes are about __ by __ in size:
questions.explanation: In prokaryotes, ribosomes are about 15 nm by 20 nm in size.

questions.question: Prokaryotic flagella are __ from eukaryotic flagella:
questions.explanation: The prokaryotic bacteria also possess flagella but these are structurally different from that of the eukaryotic flagella.

questions.question: Which function of the cell wall involves cell communication?
questions.explanation: Cell wall also helps in cell-to-cell interaction and provides barrier to undesirable macromolecules.

questions.question: Which is an example of an inclusion body in prokaryotes?
questions.explanation: Inclusion bodies include phosphate granules, cyanophycean granules and glycogen granules.

questions.question: Eukaryotic cells possess a variety of complex locomotory and __ structures:
questions.explanation: In addition, eukaryotic cells have a variety of complex locomotory and cytoskeletal structures.

questions.question: Potato is an example where amyloplasts store:
questions.explanation: Amyloplasts store carbohydrates (starch), e.g., potato.

questions.question: Chloroplasts are lens-shaped, oval, spherical, discoid or even ribbon-like organelles having variable length of:
questions.explanation: These are lens-shaped, oval, spherical, discoid or even ribbon-like organelles having variable length (5-10µm) and width (2-4µm).

questions.question: The width of chloroplasts is:
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts have variable length (5-10µm) and width (2-4µm).

questions.question: Number of chloroplasts per cell in the mesophyll is:
questions.explanation: Their number varies from 1 per cell of the Chlamydomonas, a green alga to 20-40 per cell in the mesophyll.

questions.question: Which process occurs in the nucleus?
questions.explanation: The nucleus not only controls the activities of organelles but also plays a major role in heredity.

questions.question: The ratio of protein and lipid in cell membrane varies in different cell types. This is because:

options.B: Different cells have different types and amounts of membrane proteins
options.D: Proteins are absent in some membranes

questions.explanation: Later, biochemical investigation clearly revealed that the cell membranes also possess protein and carbohydrate. The ratio of protein and lipid varies considerably in different cell types.

questions.question: The cell theory created the concept that:

options.B: Integrity of cellular organisation is necessary for life
options.C: Cells can arise from non-living matter
options.D: All organisms have same number of cells

questions.explanation: Cell theory also created a sense of mystery around living phenomena — the requirement of integrity of cellular organisation for living phenomena to be demonstrated or observed.

questions.question: Various chemical reactions occur in the cytoplasm to keep the cell in the:
questions.explanation: Various chemical reactions occur in the cytoplasm to keep the cell in the 'living state'.

questions.question: What makes the organisation of prokaryotic cells fundamentally similar despite their wide variety of shapes and functions?

options.B: All have similar cell envelopes and basic structure

questions.explanation: The organisation of the prokaryotic cell is fundamentally similar even though prokaryotes exhibit a wide variety of shapes and functions.

questions.question: In which type of transport does a carrier protein facilitate movement of polar molecules across the membrane?
questions.explanation: Polar molecules require a carrier protein of the membrane to facilitate their transport across the membrane — this is facilitated diffusion.

questions.question: The cis and trans faces of the Golgi organelle are:
questions.explanation: The cis and the trans faces of the organelle are entirely different, but interconnected.

questions.question: The number of mitochondria per cell is variable depending on:
questions.explanation: The number of mitochondria per cell is variable depending on the physiological activity of the cells.

questions.question: Ribosomes are __ by any membrane:
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are composed of ribonucleic acid (RNA) and proteins and are not surrounded by any membrane.

questions.question: Eukaryotic cells possess an organised nucleus with:
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells possess an organised nucleus with a nuclear envelope.

questions.question: Centrosomes are surrounded by amorphous:
questions.explanation: Centrioles are surrounded by amorphous pericentriolar materials.

questions.question: Chemical studies on cell membrane, especially in human RBCs, enabled scientists to deduce the possible structure of:
questions.explanation: Chemical studies on the cell membrane, especially in human red blood cells (RBCs), enabled the scientists to deduce the possible structure of plasma membrane.

questions.question: Which character distinguishes plant cells from prokaryotes at the organelle level?

options.A: Plant cells have ribosomes, prokaryotes don't
options.B: Plant cells have membrane-bound organelles, prokaryotes don't
options.C: Plant cells have DNA, prokaryotes don't
options.D: Plant cells have cell wall, prokaryotes don't

questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells (including plants) have extensive compartmentalisation through membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotes lack such membrane-bound organelles.

questions.question: Virchow modified the hypothesis of Schleiden and Schwann to give the cell theory a __ shape:
questions.explanation: He (Virchow) modified the hypothesis of Schleiden and Schwann to give the cell theory a final shape.

questions.question: The shape of a cell may vary with:
questions.explanation: The shape of the cell may vary with the function they perform.

questions.question: The ribosomes of a polysome translate the mRNA into:
questions.explanation: The ribosomes of a polysome translate the mRNA into proteins.

questions.question: Which enzymes are found in lysosomes?
questions.explanation: The isolated lysosomal vesicles have been found to be very rich in almost all types of hydrolytic enzymes (hydrolases – lipases, proteases, carbohydrases).

questions.question: In eukaryotes, the outer membrane of nuclear envelope:

options.B: Remains continuous with ER and bears ribosomes

questions.explanation: The outer membrane usually remains continuous with the endoplasmic reticulum and also bears ribosomes on it.

questions.question: Plastids are large and therefore:

options.A: Cannot be seen under light microscope
options.B: Are easily observed under the microscope

questions.explanation: These (plastids) are easily observed under the microscope as they are large.

questions.question: Which molecules can move across the plasma membrane by simple diffusion?
questions.explanation: Neutral solutes may move across the membrane by the process of simple diffusion along the concentration gradient.

questions.question: In bacteria, unique phenotypic characters from plasmid DNA include:
questions.explanation: The plasmid DNA confers certain unique phenotypic characters to such bacteria. One such character is resistance to antibiotics.

questions.question: Chromosomes are visible only in:
questions.explanation: Every chromosome (visible only in dividing cells) essentially has a primary constriction or the centromere.

questions.question: Which organelle contains plasmodesmata for intercellular connection?
questions.explanation: The cell wall and middle lamellae may be traversed by plasmodesmata which connect the cytoplasm of neighbouring cells.

questions.question: The stroma of chloroplast contains small, double-stranded circular DNA molecules and:
questions.explanation: The stroma also contains small, double-stranded circular DNA molecules and ribosomes. The ribosomes of the chloroplasts are smaller (70S).

questions.question: Mitochondria possess their own DNA, RNA, and ribosomes, suggesting they may have been:
questions.explanation: The matrix possesses single circular DNA molecule, a few RNA molecules, ribosomes (70S). These features suggest evolutionary origin from prokaryotes (endosymbiont theory).

questions.question: The percentage of lipids in human erythrocyte membrane is approximately:
questions.explanation: The membrane of the erythrocyte has approximately 52 per cent protein and 40 per cent lipids.

questions.question: The cytoskeleton is present in the:
questions.explanation: An elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures is present in the cytoplasm, collectively referred to as the cytoskeleton.

questions.question: What was emphasised by the cell theory?

options.B: Unity underlying diversity — cellular organisation of all life

questions.explanation: It is the cell theory that emphasised the unity underlying this diversity of forms, i.e., the cellular organisation of all life forms.

questions.question: Plant cells have __ which help them withstand turgor pressure:
questions.explanation: Plant cells have cell walls which give shape and provide structural support. The cell wall helps the plant cell withstand osmotic pressure (turgor).

questions.question: The 'S' value is a measure of density and size and is determined by:
questions.explanation: 'S' (Svedberg's Unit) stands for the sedimentation coefficient; it is indirectly a measure of density and size.

questions.question: The hub of centrioles is connected with tubules of peripheral triplets by:
questions.explanation: The hub is connected with tubules of the peripheral triplets by radial spokes made of protein.

questions.question: The nucleoplasm contains nucleolus and:
questions.explanation: The nuclear matrix or the nucleoplasm contains nucleolus and chromatin.

questions.question: Which process does NOT require energy?
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water by diffusion, which is passive and does not require energy.

questions.question: Plasmid DNA is used in biotechnology to monitor:
questions.explanation: This plasmid DNA is used to monitor bacterial transformation with foreign DNA.

questions.question: Golgi cisternae are stacked:
questions.explanation: These (cisternae) are stacked parallel to each other.

questions.question: Intergranal thylakoids are another name for:
questions.explanation: Thylakoids are arranged in stacks called grana or the intergranal thylakoids. Stroma lamellae connect thylakoids of different grana.

questions.question: Large central vacuole is present in:
questions.explanation: Plant cells possess cell walls, plastids and a large central vacuole which are absent in animal cells.

questions.question: Bacteria can be classified into Gram positive and Gram negative based on differences in:

options.B: Cell envelopes and response to staining procedure

questions.explanation: Bacteria can be classified into two groups on the basis of the differences in the cell envelopes and the manner in which they respond to the staining procedure developed by Gram.

questions.question: The middle lamella holds neighbouring cells together like:
questions.explanation: The middle lamella is a layer mainly of calcium pectate which holds or glues the different neighbouring cells together.

questions.question: The outer membrane of mitochondria forms:

options.C: Continuous limiting boundary of the organelle

questions.explanation: The outer membrane forms the continuous limiting boundary of the organelle.

questions.question: The fluid mosaic model is also known as:
questions.explanation: The model was proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972), hence also known as Singer-Nicolson model or fluid mosaic model.

questions.question: Chloroplasts contain double-stranded circular DNA in their:
questions.explanation: The stroma of the chloroplast contains enzymes required for the synthesis of carbohydrates and proteins. It also contains small, double-stranded circular DNA molecules and ribosomes.

questions.question: Sieve tube cells of vascular plants are mature cells that:
questions.explanation: Some mature cells even lack nucleus, e.g., erythrocytes of many mammals and sieve tube cells of vascular plants.

questions.question: Human cells have how many pairs of chromosomes?
questions.explanation: A single human cell has approximately two metre long thread of DNA distributed among its forty six (twenty three pairs) chromosomes.

questions.question: Which organelle is common in eukaryotes but NOT considered part of endomembrane system?
questions.explanation: Peroxisomes, along with mitochondria and chloroplasts, are not considered part of the endomembrane system because their functions are not coordinated with ER, Golgi, lysosomes and vacuoles.

questions.question: Cells differ greatly in their shape. They may be:

options.B: Disc-like, polygonal, columnar, cuboid, thread-like, or irregular

questions.explanation: Cells also vary greatly in their shape. They may be disc-like, polygonal, columnar, cuboid, thread-like, or even irregular.

questions.question: Which of the following is found only in eukaryotes and NOT in prokaryotes?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles like Golgi apparatus. They only have ribosomes (non-membrane-bound) among organelle-like structures.

questions.question: Lysosomes are also called 'suicide bags' because they:

options.B: Contain hydrolytic enzymes that can digest the cell itself

questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain hydrolytic enzymes that are capable of digesting carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids, hence can destroy the cell itself — earning them the name 'suicide bags'.

questions.question: Osmosis is specifically the movement of:

options.A: All molecules from high to low concentration

questions.explanation: Movement of water by diffusion is called osmosis.

questions.question: Anything less than a complete structure of a cell does not ensure:
questions.explanation: Anything less than a complete structure of a cell does not ensure independent living. Hence, cell is the fundamental structural and functional unit.

questions.question: Where are the Golgi cisternae concentrically arranged?
questions.explanation: The Golgi cisternae are concentrically arranged near the nucleus with distinct convex cis or the forming face and concave trans or the maturing face.

questions.question: The peripheral doublets of cilia/flagella axoneme are interconnected by:
questions.explanation: The peripheral doublets are also interconnected by linkers.

questions.question: The length of mitochondria is:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria typically have a diameter of 0.2-1.0µm (average 0.5µm) and length 1.0-4.1µm.

questions.question: The nuclear matrix is also called:
questions.explanation: The nuclear matrix or the nucleoplasm contains nucleolus and chromatin.

questions.question: In Amoeba, the contractile vacuole helps in:
questions.explanation: In Amoeba, the contractile vacuole is important for osmoregulation and excretion.

questions.question: The ER divides the intracellular space into how many compartments?
questions.explanation: ER divides the intracellular space into two distinct compartments — luminal (inside ER) and extra luminal (cytoplasm) compartments.

questions.question: Materials released from the trans face of Golgi include:

options.B: Proteins modified in the Golgi cisternae

questions.explanation: A number of proteins synthesised by ribosomes on the endoplasmic reticulum are modified in the cisternae of the golgi apparatus before they are released from its trans face.

questions.question: Theodore Schwann (1839) studied different types of animal cells and concluded:

options.A: All animal cells have cell walls
options.B: Animals and plants are composed of cells

questions.explanation: Schwann proposed the hypothesis that the bodies of animals and plants are composed of cells and products of cells.

questions.question: Prokaryotic 70S ribosome is made of:
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S, made of 50S and 30S subunits. (Note: 50+30 ≠ 70 because S values are not additive — they depend on shape and density.)

questions.question: The stroma lamellae connect thylakoids of:
questions.explanation: Flat membranous tubules called the stroma lamellae connecting the thylakoids of the different grana.

questions.question: The term 'selectively permeable' for the plasma membrane means:

options.A: It allows all molecules to pass
options.B: It allows no molecules to pass
options.C: It allows only selected molecules to pass

questions.explanation: The membrane is selectively permeable to some molecules present on either side of it — meaning it controls which molecules can pass through.

questions.question: The ribosomes in mitochondrial matrix are:
questions.explanation: The matrix possesses single circular DNA molecule, a few RNA molecules, ribosomes (70S) and the components required for the synthesis of proteins.

questions.question: Cilia/flagella are covered with:
questions.explanation: The electron microscopic study shows that cilia/flagella are covered with plasma membrane.

questions.question: Unicellular organisms are capable of:

options.B: Independent existence and performing essential functions of life

questions.explanation: Unicellular organisms are capable of (i) independent existence and (ii) performing the essential functions of life.

questions.question: The adjacent triplets in centrioles are:
questions.explanation: Each of the peripheral fibril is a triplet. The adjacent triplets are also linked.
